title: SEGMENT 15 — The MRI truth — atrophy continued; this slowed decline, didn't reverse it

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — The MRI truth — atrophy continued; this slowed decline, didn't reverse it
I have, until now, emphasized the encouraging findings of the paper.
Now I have to tell you the hard part.
Because the milasen paper, to its enormous credit, did not hide or downplay this finding, and we should not either.
In addition to the clinical assessments of behavior, motor function, and seizures, Mila underwent serial MRI scans of the brain during the treatment period.
The purpose of these scans was to monitor whether the underlying neurodegeneration — the brain atrophy that had been documented before treatment — was changing in response to the drug.
The hope, ideally, would have been that the atrophy would slow or even reverse.
The reality is what the MRIs showed.
Across the first year of treatment, Mila's brain continued to lose volume.
The atrophy progressed.

Not, perhaps, at the same alarming rate it might have without treatment — that is hard to know for certain, because there is no untreated control Mila to compare to, and the natural history of late-stage CLN7 varies between patients.
But the atrophy did not stop.
It did not reverse.
The neurons that were dying continued to die.
The brain that had already shrunk continued to shrink.
I want you to sit with that for a moment, because it is the central honest finding of this paper, and it shapes the interpretation of everything else.
The seizures got better.
The drug was doing something — something measurable, something clinically meaningful, something that improved a major and disabling symptom of the disease.
But the underlying process — the progressive death of neurons due to lysosomal dysfunction — continued.
Milasen reduced one consequence of the disease.
It did not stop the disease itself.
Why might that be?
There are several plausible explanations, none of which are mutually exclusive, and none of which we can definitively distinguish from a single patient.

One possibility is that by the time treatment began — at age six, with substantial brain atrophy already present — too many neurons had already been damaged or lost for any drug to reverse the trajectory.
The drug could only act on cells that were still alive.
Cells that had already died were beyond its reach.
The window for meaningful intervention in a neurodegenerative disease may close earlier than we would like to admit.
Another possibility is that the drug was not reaching the entire affected brain in adequate concentrations.
We discussed in Segment 13 that intrathecally delivered ASOs distribute unevenly within the CNS, with the brain getting roughly one-third the exposure of the spinal cord.
Even at the three-times-nusinersen dose used for Mila, some regions of her brain may have received insufficient drug to drive meaningful splicing correction in neurons.
A third possibility is that the splicing correction itself, even at maximum, was incomplete.
In Mila's fibroblasts, milasen tripled the proportion of correctly spliced transcripts, from eight percent to thirty-four percent.
That is a substantial improvement, but it is not a full restoration.
The treated cells were still producing a majority of mis-spliced transcripts.
The amount of functional MFSD8 protein being produced from her SVA-carrying allele, even with milasen present, was still well below what a healthy person produces from a normal allele.

Combined with whatever functional product might come from her c.1102G>C allele — which the literature suggests produces some small amount of partially functional protein — the total functional MFSD8 in her cells, on treatment, was probably still well below normal.
Perhaps enough to reduce seizures and stabilize some functions, not enough to halt or reverse the underlying neurodegeneration.
And of course it is also possible that some combination of all three explanations is correct.
Probably is correct.
The clinical implication of the MRI finding is sobering.

It tells us that even when an individualized ASO works — even when it does exactly what it was designed to do, with measurable benefit on at least one major symptom — that benefit may not equate to a cure.
The disease may continue to progress, more slowly perhaps, but still toward the same ultimate endpoint.
For a family considering an individualized therapy for a child with a fatal disease, this is not a small consideration.
The drug may extend life.
It may improve quality of life.
It may reduce symptom burden.
It may not, in the end, save the patient.
This is the moment in the lecture where I want to remind you of something I said in Segment 1.
Mila died in February of 2021, two and a half years after the publication of the paper, at age ten.
The MRI findings predicted, in some sense, what eventually happened.
The brain volume loss that was visible on those serial scans continued through the years after the paper was published.
The disease, slowed but not stopped, eventually reached the endpoint that severe neurodegenerative diseases reach.
The drug did not fail.
By any reasonable measure of what it was designed to do, it succeeded — it corrected splicing, it reduced seizures, it stabilized some clinical measures, it was tolerated safely.
But succeeding at what the drug was designed to do is not the same as curing the disease.
And we should not, in our enthusiasm for the platform's promise, lose sight of that distinction.
